├── AGENTS.md                          # cross-tool fallback (also read by copilot)
└── .github/
    ├── copilot-instructions.md        # always-loaded project instructions
    ├── instructions/
    │   └── tests.instructions.md      # path-scoped via applyTo frontmatter
    ├── prompts/
    │   └── refactor.prompt.md         # /refactor reusable prompt
    ├── chatmodes/
    │   └── reviewer.chatmode.md       # custom chat mode (chat / edit / agent)
    ├── agents/
    │   └── code-reviewer.agent.md     # custom cloud agent (2026)
    └── skills/
        └── deploy/
            └── SKILL.md               # agent skill (workspace-scoped)

# Org/enterprise scope (separate repo): .github-private/agents/<name>.agent.md

~/.copilot/skills/                     # personal skills (install via `gh skill install`)
~/.agents/skills/                      # cross-tool fallback skills dir
VS Code user settings (~/.config/Code/User/settings.json on Linux,
                       ~/Library/Application Support/Code/User on Mac)
└── settings.json                      # personal Copilot prefs, MCP, model picks

# Org-level instructions: GitHub.com org settings (no on-disk file)
# Copilot also discovers skills from .claude/skills/ and .agents/skills/

A path-scoped tests.instructions.md:

---
applyTo: "**/*.test.{ts,tsx}"
---
Use vitest. Prefer `describe` + `it`. No snapshot tests.
your-project/
│
├── AGENTS.md                          # cross-tool fallback (also read by cursor)
├── .cursorignore                      # files cursor never reads
├── .cursorrules                       # legacy single-file rules (deprecated v2.2)
└── .cursor/
    ├── rules/
    │   ├── always.mdc                 # alwaysApply: true
    │   ├── auto.mdc                   # auto-attached by description
    │   └── manual.mdc                 # invoked with @ruleName
    ├── commands/
    │   └── ship.md                    # saved prompt shortcuts (/ship)
    ├── skills/                        # on-demand workflows (Cursor 2.4, Jan 2026)
    │   └── deploy/SKILL.md
    ├── mcp.json                       # project MCP servers
    └── hooks.json                     # lifecycle hooks (Cursor 2.x)

~/.cursor/                              # user-global, merged with project
├── rules/, commands/, skills/
└── hooks.json

# User rules also editable in Cursor Settings → Rules
# Subagents (Cursor 2.4) ship alongside skills, configured via the same .cursor/ surface

A .cursor/hooks.json:

{
  "version": 1,
  "hooks": {
    "beforeShellExecution": [
      { "command": "sh -lc 'block-dangerous-commands.sh'" }
    ],
    "afterFileEdit": [
      { "command": "sh -lc 'npm run lint:fix'" }
    ]
  }
}
your-project/
│
├── AGENTS.md                          # cross-tool fallback (Antigravity v1.20.3+)
├── GEMINI.md                          # Antigravity-specific; takes precedence over AGENTS.md
└── .agent/
    ├── rules/                         # split workspace rules into multiple files
    │   ├── code-style.md
    │   └── testing.md
    ├── skills/
    │   └── deploy-staging/SKILL.md    # workspace skill (directory-based package)
    └── workflows/                     # step-by-step automation recipes
        └── release.md                 # supports `// turbo` for auto-run

subdir/
└── AGENTS.md                          # nested files apply to that folder only
                                       # (toggle: Settings → Agent → load nested)

~/.gemini/                              # user-global
├── GEMINI.md                          # personal Antigravity rules
├── AGENTS.md                          # personal cross-tool rules (v1.20.3+)
└── antigravity/
    └── skills/                        # global skills (workspace overrides by name)

Note: .agent/ (singular) is Antigravity’s own dir. .agents/skills/ (plural) is the cross-tool skills fallback that Copilot reads. Different things.

Exercise

Ask your AI:

From the glossary, quiz me on skill, subagent, hook, MCP, and system prompt. One at a time. Tell me what I miss.

Then: “Pick the term I fumbled worst on. Add one to this project that would actually be useful.”

Every tool loads one markdown file into every session. That’s your foundation. Everything else is optional.

The foundation file

Named differently per tool — CLAUDE.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, .cursor/rules/project.mdc, GEMINI.md — contents are the same shape. Put stuff in here when the AI doesn’t already know it:

# my-app

Next.js 15 + Postgres. Playwright for e2e.

## Things you'd miss without me telling you
- Migrations in `db/migrations/` are append-only.
- `src/legacy/` is frozen unless I say otherwise.
- Auth always goes through `requireUser()`.
- Keep files under 500 lines.

Don’t hand-write it. /init or “draft a short CLAUDE.md from this repo.”

The rest of the surface

Everything below is optional — you’d add it when you hit a specific pain. You don’t need to remember the file paths. If you can say the word (hook, skill, subagent, permission, MCP, rule), the AI can set it up for you.

your-project/
│
├── CLAUDE.md                          # always-loaded project instructions
├── .mcp.json                          # project MCP servers (committed)
├── .worktreeinclude                   # files to copy into git worktrees
└── .claude/
    ├── settings.json                  # permissions, hooks, env (team-shared)
    ├── settings.local.json            # personal overrides (gitignored)
    ├── rules/                         # split workspace rules (complement CLAUDE.md)
    ├── agents/
    │   └── reviewer.md                # custom subagent (Task tool can spawn)
    ├── commands/                      # /command shortcuts (older)
    ├── skills/                        # auto-invoked workflows (preferred over commands now)
    │   └── kata/SKILL.md
    ├── hooks/                         # shell scripts on tool events
    ├── output-styles/                 # response style presets (e.g. teaching.md)
    └── agent-memory/                  # Claude-written persistent agent memory
        └── <agent-name>/MEMORY.md

~/.claude.json                          # top-level user config (distinct from below)
~/.claude/                              # user-global, same shape as project
├── CLAUDE.md                          # personal preferences across all projects
├── settings.json                      # global permissions / deny lists
├── keybindings.json                   # custom keybindings
├── rules/, agents/, commands/, skills/, hooks/, output-styles/, agent-memory/
├── plugins/                           # installed marketplace plugins
└── projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md  # auto-memory per project

Note: Commands and skills are now the same mechanism. For new workflows, use skills/.

A starter .claude/settings.json:

{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": ["Bash(npm run *)", "Read", "Edit", "Write"],
    "deny": ["Bash(rm -rf *)"]
  }
}
your-project/
│
├── AGENTS.md                          # cross-tool fallback (also read by copilot)
└── .github/
    ├── copilot-instructions.md        # always-loaded project instructions
    ├── instructions/
    │   └── tests.instructions.md      # path-scoped via applyTo frontmatter
    ├── prompts/
    │   └── refactor.prompt.md         # /refactor reusable prompt
    ├── chatmodes/
    │   └── reviewer.chatmode.md       # custom chat mode (chat / edit / agent)
    ├── agents/
    │   └── code-reviewer.agent.md     # custom cloud agent (2026)
    └── skills/
        └── deploy/
            └── SKILL.md               # agent skill (workspace-scoped)

# Org/enterprise scope (separate repo): .github-private/agents/<name>.agent.md

~/.copilot/skills/                     # personal skills (install via `gh skill install`)
~/.agents/skills/                      # cross-tool fallback skills dir
VS Code user settings (~/.config/Code/User/settings.json on Linux,
                       ~/Library/Application Support/Code/User on Mac)
└── settings.json                      # personal Copilot prefs, MCP, model picks

# Org-level instructions: GitHub.com org settings (no on-disk file)
# Copilot also discovers skills from .claude/skills/ and .agents/skills/

A path-scoped tests.instructions.md:

---
applyTo: "**/*.test.{ts,tsx}"
---
Use vitest. Prefer `describe` + `it`. No snapshot tests.
your-project/
│
├── AGENTS.md                          # cross-tool fallback (also read by cursor)
├── .cursorignore                      # files cursor never reads
├── .cursorrules                       # legacy single-file rules (deprecated v2.2)
└── .cursor/
    ├── rules/
    │   ├── always.mdc                 # alwaysApply: true
    │   ├── auto.mdc                   # auto-attached by description
    │   └── manual.mdc                 # invoked with @ruleName
    ├── commands/
    │   └── ship.md                    # saved prompt shortcuts (/ship)
    ├── skills/                        # on-demand workflows (Cursor 2.4, Jan 2026)
    │   └── deploy/SKILL.md
    ├── mcp.json                       # project MCP servers
    └── hooks.json                     # lifecycle hooks (Cursor 2.x)

~/.cursor/                              # user-global, merged with project
├── rules/, commands/, skills/
└── hooks.json

# User rules also editable in Cursor Settings → Rules
# Subagents (Cursor 2.4) ship alongside skills, configured via the same .cursor/ surface

A .cursor/hooks.json:

{
  "version": 1,
  "hooks": {
    "beforeShellExecution": [
      { "command": "sh -lc 'block-dangerous-commands.sh'" }
    ],
    "afterFileEdit": [
      { "command": "sh -lc 'npm run lint:fix'" }
    ]
  }
}
your-project/
│
├── AGENTS.md                          # cross-tool fallback (Antigravity v1.20.3+)
├── GEMINI.md                          # Antigravity-specific; takes precedence over AGENTS.md
└── .agent/
    ├── rules/                         # split workspace rules into multiple files
    │   ├── code-style.md
    │   └── testing.md
    ├── skills/
    │   └── deploy-staging/SKILL.md    # workspace skill (directory-based package)
    └── workflows/                     # step-by-step automation recipes
        └── release.md                 # supports `// turbo` for auto-run

subdir/
└── AGENTS.md                          # nested files apply to that folder only
                                       # (toggle: Settings → Agent → load nested)

~/.gemini/                              # user-global
├── GEMINI.md                          # personal Antigravity rules
├── AGENTS.md                          # personal cross-tool rules (v1.20.3+)
└── antigravity/
    └── skills/                        # global skills (workspace overrides by name)

Note: .agent/ (singular) is Antigravity’s own dir. .agents/skills/ (plural) is the cross-tool skills fallback that Copilot reads. Different things.

Exercise

Go to the glossary. Tell your AI:

Quiz me on skill, subagent, hook, MCP, and system prompt. One term at a time. Tell me what I missed after each.

Then pick the term you felt shakiest on and say: “Add one of those to this project that would actually be useful.” See what files appear — that’s the surface you didn’t have to memorize.

Think about what you’d do in each scenario. Then click to reveal.

Why is my session already using tokens before I’ve typed anything?

Every tool preloads stuff for you. A bare session (no MCP, minimal config) runs roughly 5-25k tokens depending on the tool:

  • Gemini CLI: ~6k
  • Antigravity: ~10k+
  • Claude Code: ~20k
  • Codex CLI: ~21-22k
  • Cursor, Windsurf, Copilot: not publicly measured, but leaked prompts suggest a similar band

Add MCP and big instruction files and it climbs fast:

  • Config files: CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursor/rules/*, GEMINI.md. Most people have 0 or 1 small file, which adds maybe 1-3k. A sprawling CLAUDE.md can add 10k+.
  • Memory files: user-level notes (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, ~/.gemini/GEMINI.md). Usually small.
  • MCP servers: 5-10k per server typical, 15-20k for heavy ones.

Every MCP server you leave enabled is rent you pay on every session. Turn off what you don’t need.

How do I see what’s in my context right now, and how much I’ve used?

Every tool has a way, usually one command or a hover.

  • Claude Code: /context breaks it down by category (system prompt, memory, tools, MCP, messages). /status shows the token count and model.
  • Cursor / VS Code Copilot: a circle indicator shows up near the chat input once a conversation starts. Hover it for the token breakdown.
  • Codex CLI: /status shows session usage.
  • Gemini CLI: /stats for usage, /memory show to see the concatenated memory.

Worth checking when the AI starts acting off. If the window is getting full, context bloat is probably the reason, and clearing will help more than any correction you type.

Your token usage is high and the AI is starting to miss things: forgetting earlier decisions, repeating itself, getting sloppier. What do you do?

Reset the context. Three ways, depending on what you need:

  • Clear / new. Fastest. Wipes the conversation; files on disk stay. /clear in Claude Code and Gemini CLI, /clear or /new in Copilot CLI, “New Chat” in Cursor and VS Code Copilot.
  • Compact. Keeps continuity by summarizing older history into a shorter form. Use when you can’t afford to lose the thread. /compact [focus] in Claude Code, /compact in Copilot CLI, /compress in Gemini CLI.
  • Document, then clear. Write a handoff file first (“Write HANDOFF.md: what we tried, what worked, what’s left”), then clear. Next session opens with “Read HANDOFF.md and continue.” Best for long tasks you’ll return to.

Model attention gets worse as context fills up, so doing nothing is the worst option.

The AI keeps suggesting fixes that don’t work, and you’ve corrected it twice. What do you do?

Give it better inputs. It’s probably working from stale training data, vague symptoms, or a mental model of your system that’s wrong. A few things that actually move the needle:

  • Have it add logs and run again, then read the output.
  • Point it at a REPL or let it run the failing command itself.
  • Paste a screenshot of the error, UI glitch, or failing test output.
  • Connect a relevant MCP (Sentry, Linear, your DB, Chrome DevTools).
  • Tell it to search the web for the error + “2026”; library behavior changes fast.
  • Give it the official docs URL or an up-to-date reference and tell it to read that specifically.
  • Be more concrete: “what exactly are you trying and why do you think it will work this time?”

Repeating “still broken” without new inputs just burns tokens. New information unsticks it.

You’re mid-task, the window is getting heavy, but you need continuity. What do you do?

Compact with a focus. /compact focus on the auth refactor, drop the test debugging in Claude Code. /compact in Copilot CLI. /compress in Gemini CLI. This summarizes older turns into a short form so you can keep going. Do it on purpose at ~50-60% full, not when auto-compact kicks in at 80% with no steering.

You realize the AI took a wrong turn ten messages ago. What do you do?

Rewind to that point. /rewind or Esc+Esc in Claude Code opens a checkpoint picker (conversation only, or conversation + files). In Cursor, VS Code Copilot, and Windsurf, each message has a restore button right next to it; click it to undo chat and file changes back to that point.

You want to try a risky approach without losing where you are. What do you do?

Fork the session. /branch in Claude Code, or claude --resume <id> --fork-session. The original thread stays untouched. Few tools have this natively. Files are shared across branches, so file writes still leak. Use it for “let me try this totally different thing and come back if it doesn’t work.”

You’re wrapping up for the day and want to resume tomorrow. What do you do?

Write a handoff file. “Write HANDOFF.md: what you tried, what worked, what didn’t, next steps.” Tomorrow: start a fresh session with “Read HANDOFF.md and continue.” Beats claude --continue into a long stale thread. Commits help too: a new session can read the last few commits to catch up cheaply.

The AI keeps making the same mistake across multiple sessions. What do you do?

Get it out of chat and into the instructions file. In Claude Code, start a message with # and it prompts where to save (project CLAUDE.md, user ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, or auto-memory). In Cursor, say “Turn this into a rule.” In Copilot, “Add this to AGENTS.md” (or .github/copilot-instructions.md). If you correct the same thing twice, the fix belongs in the file, not the conversation.

You’re about to start a hard, multi-step task. What do you do?

Three levels, escalate as needed:

  • Start with “interview me.” “Before you write anything, interview me to understand the task. Ask me questions one at a time until you’re confident.” Low effort, high leverage. Usually enough on its own, and way better than diving straight to code.
  • Add plan mode if you want a clean implementation session. Plan mode (/plan or Shift+Tab in Claude Code, Plan tab in Cursor) produces a concrete plan without touching code. Save it as PLAN.md, clear, and start a fresh session that loads only the plan. Worth it when you expect implementation to get long.
  • For a huge process, use a spec-driven skill. Spec-driven workflows (write a full spec, break into steps, implement step by step with checkpoints) are well-suited for a reusable skill. Set it up once and invoke it by name for every big task.

A session with even a short interview beats a session that dove straight in. A session with a clean plan beats one carrying the full back-and-forth.

The AI needs context that isn’t in your codebase (design docs, tickets, team discussion). What do you do?

Paste it, link it, or pull it in through MCP. For a one-off, paste the relevant Slack thread, a screenshot, or a URL and ask the agent to fetch it. For repeat access, connect an MCP server if you can: Linear, GitHub, Notion, Confluence, Obsidian, and Slack all ship them in 2026. Heads up: locked-down company systems (Slack, Jira, internal wikis) often won’t let you connect a third-party MCP server, so copy-paste is frequently the only option there. Turn off MCP servers you’re not using this session; each one typically spends 5-10k tokens on its tool schemas.

You open a fresh session and want to get caught up quickly. What do you do?

Feed it a high-signal source instead of re-explaining. A few options, in order of effort:

  • Recent commits. “Read the last 5 commits and tell me where we left off.” Cheap and works best when you commit small and often.
  • A handoff file you wrote yesterday. HANDOFF.md, NOTES.md, or a decisions/ folder of ADRs. The in-repo pattern that’s become standard alongside AGENTS.md.
  • Your own notes system. If you keep a second brain in Obsidian or Notion, connect it via MCP and let the agent query your design docs, running notes, or meeting minutes directly.

The pattern: build one reliable source your future self (or future agent) can read instead of paging you.

Context is everything the model sees

Every message you send, the AI receives one big bundle called the context. Your chat input is only a small piece of it. The bundle also includes:

  • System prompt: the tool’s built-in instructions plus your config files (CLAUDE.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, .cursor/rules/, GEMINI.md).
  • Conversation history: every message in this session, yours and the AI’s.
  • Attached files: anything you @-mentioned, pasted, or the agent opened with Read.
  • Tool output: results from every bash command, grep, web fetch, or MCP call the agent ran.
  • Memory: notes the tool persisted from past sessions about you or the project.

All of it counts against the context window (typically 200k to 1M tokens in 2026). When it fills up, tools either auto-compact (summarize older turns) or start dropping the oldest content. Both lose information.

See what’s in yours

In Claude Code, type /context to see a breakdown by category.

In VS Code Copilot, hover the token circle at the bottom right of the chat input.

Control it

  • Clear when you switch tasks: /clear in Claude Code, “New Chat” in Copilot/Cursor.
  • Compact when you want to keep going but trim history: /compact in Claude Code.
  • Be selective with file attachments. Reading a 10k-line file spends 40k+ tokens.
  • Use subagents for heavy lifts (big research, deep reviews) so the main thread stays clean.

Exercise

  1. Run /context (or your tool’s equivalent) right now. Note the numbers.
  2. Ask the AI to read three large files in your repo. Run /context again. See how much grew.
  3. /clear and start fresh. Confirm you’re back to baseline.

Your context window is a budget

Every session has a fixed ceiling. Anthropic models today are at 200k to 1M tokens. That sounds like a lot until you start spending it:

  • A single 1000-line file: ~4k tokens.
  • Ten tool outputs (grep, read, bash): ~15k tokens.
  • A long back-and-forth exchange: grows every turn.
  • Your config files and system prompt: ~20k before you type anything.

When the budget runs out, the tool either auto-compacts old turns into a summary or starts evicting them. Both are lossy. And well before you hit the ceiling, models start missing things. Attention gets worse as context grows. You’ve probably felt this: the AI forgets what you said 30 messages ago, or repeats a mistake it just fixed.

See your burn rate

In Claude Code, type /context to see exactly what you’ve spent.

In VS Code Copilot, the token count is tucked into the bottom right of the chat input.

Spend it wisely

  • Short sessions beat long ones. When a task wraps, /clear and start fresh.
  • Don’t read what you don’t need. Pointing the agent at a 5000-line file for a 10-line change is expensive and dilutes its focus.
  • Delegate to subagents. A subagent runs in its own budget and returns a summary, keeping your main thread cheap.
  • Compact before you’re desperate. Running /compact at 70% full gives you room. Waiting until 95% full means the summary is rushed.

Exercise

  1. Start a session. Run /context and note the starting number.
  2. Do a normal chunk of work: a few edits, a test run, some chat. Run /context again.
  3. Ask your AI: “Which of the files I’ve read in this session can you forget? What would help most to clear from context?” See what it suggests.

Context is a desk

Everything the AI has seen in this session is sitting on its desk: files it read, commands it ran, stuff you said five messages ago, and the failed attempt from an hour back. The more stuff piles up, the harder it is to find the important thing. Models don’t read every token equally. Things in the middle of a long context get less attention (often called “context rot”). Old failed attempts confuse new ones.

Good habits keep the desk clean.

Habits that work

  • Finish a task, then clear. /clear in Claude Code, “New Chat” in Cursor/Copilot. A session that carries three finished tasks plus the current one is noisier than a fresh one.
  • Start over when you’ve gone sideways. If the AI misunderstood 10 turns ago and you’ve been arguing since, clear and restate the goal from scratch. Faster than untangling.
  • Use subagents for side quests. Need to research a library, review a PR, or read 20 files? Spin a subagent. It works in its own context and gives you back just the conclusion.
  • Be picky about attachments. Attaching a whole repo “just in case” is like dumping every file on the desk before asking a question.

See the clutter

In Claude Code, /context shows a breakdown of what’s loaded.

In Cursor, the chat input shows a context indicator. You can also remove specific attachments from a turn before you send it.

Exercise

  1. Next time your AI does something off-base twice in a row, don’t try to correct it a third time. /clear and restate.
  2. Pick a recent session that went long. Ask: “Summarize what we accomplished in one paragraph so I can start fresh.” Paste that into a new session as your starting point.
  3. Run /context before and after a /clear. Feel the difference.

What you start with

Before you type anything, your session already holds:

  • Config files: CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursor/rules/*, GEMINI.md.
  • Memory files: user-level notes (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, ~/.gemini/GEMINI.md) and project-level.
  • Tool definitions: every built-in tool plus every MCP server’s schema (often 5-20k tokens per MCP server).
  • System prompt: the tool’s own instructions.

That’s 15-40k tokens gone before “hello.” Worth knowing because every MCP server you leave enabled is rent you pay every session.

Inspect with /context (Claude Code) or the token indicator in Cursor and Copilot.

The six moves

1. Clear (/clear, “New Chat”). Wipe the conversation. Files on disk stay. Use between unrelated tasks, or when a thread has gone sideways and you’re tired of correcting it.

2. Compact (/compact [focus] in Claude Code, /compress in Gemini CLI, /compact in Copilot CLI). Summarize older history into a short form so you can keep going. In Claude Code you can steer the summary: /compact focus on the auth refactor, drop the test noise. Tools auto-compact at ~80% full, but you get a better summary by doing it earlier and on purpose.

3. Rewind (/rewind or Esc+Esc in Claude Code, per-message rewind button in Cursor). Go back to a checkpoint. Restore the conversation, or the conversation plus files, or keep the work and just drop the messages. Useful when “let me try that again, but different.”

4. Fork (/branch or claude --resume <id> --fork-session in Claude Code). Take the current state and branch into an experiment without losing the original thread. Files are shared across branches, so writes still leak. Few tools have this natively.

5. Resume (claude --continue, codex resume --last, Cursor chat history sidebar). Pick a conversation back up where you left off.

6. Plan, then reset. Use plan mode (/plan or Shift+Tab in Claude Code, Plan tab in Cursor) to have the AI produce a concrete plan without writing anything. Save it to HANDOFF.md or PLAN.md. /clear. Start a fresh session that loads only the plan. A clean session with a precise plan outperforms a long session with accumulated corrections almost every time.

Exercise

  1. Run /context now. Note what’s already loaded before you’ve done anything.
  2. Disable one MCP server you’re not using this session. Run /context again and see the difference.
  3. In your next long task, try the plan-then-reset pattern: produce a plan, save it as HANDOFF.md, /clear, and start fresh with “Read HANDOFF.md and continue.” Compare the output to your usual long-thread approach.

Bigger windows, same problem

Sonnet 4.5 at 1M tokens retrieves accurately only ~18% of the time. GPT-class models degrade similarly. The “lost in the middle” effect means things buried in a long history get less attention than the top and bottom. Bigger context windows move the cliff out, they don’t remove it.

Treat anything past ~30-40% of the window as degraded territory. Once you notice the drop, do something about it.

Signs your context has gone stale

  • It repeats a suggestion you already tried.
  • It re-reads a file it read ten minutes ago.
  • It agrees with something that contradicts what you said earlier.
  • It reintroduces a bug you just fixed.
  • Answers get shorter, vaguer, or more generic.

When any of these show up, stop correcting and reset.

Clear vs compact

  • Clear (/clear, “New Chat”) wipes the conversation. Fast, cheap, clean. Use when your next task is unrelated, or when the thread is poisoned.
  • Compact (/compact [focus] in Claude Code, /compress in Gemini CLI) summarizes the history into a shorter form so you can keep going. Use mid-task when continuity matters. Steer the summary: /compact focus on the auth refactor. Don’t wait for auto-compact at ~80% full; a proactive compact gives you a better summary.

Plan, then reset

The highest-leverage move for a hard task: use plan mode to produce a concrete plan without writing code, save it as HANDOFF.md, clear, and start a fresh session that reads only the handoff. A clean session with a precise plan beats a long session with accumulated corrections.

  • Claude Code: /plan or Shift+Tab.
  • Cursor: Plan tab.
  • Codex CLI: /plan.

Handoff prompt: “Write HANDOFF.md: what you tried, what worked, what didn’t, and next steps. The next agent loads only this file.”

Commits as free context

Every commit is a natural checkpoint the AI can read cheaply. Instead of re-explaining a change from earlier, ask: “Read the last five commits and tell me where we left off.” Commit small and often so the diffs stay useful. gh, git log, and git diff are the cheapest, highest-signal context sources you have.

Exercise

  1. Next time the AI repeats a suggestion it already tried, stop correcting. Say “Write HANDOFF.md with what we tried and what’s next.” Then /clear and paste the handoff into a new session.
  2. On your next long session, commit whenever you finish a logical chunk. Tomorrow, open a new session and start with “Read the last 5 commits to get caught up.” Notice how much less you have to explain.

Context engineering is three things

Every session is a loop: pull in the right source material, push out what’s worth keeping, and reset before the window rots.

Pull in

The files you @-attach are the obvious source, but there are others that cost less and often help more:

  • Recent commits: “Read the last 5 commits and tell me where we left off.” Cheap. High signal.
  • PRs and issues: gh pr view 1234, gh issue view 567. The agent gets the discussion, not just your summary.
  • Notes app: point Obsidian, Notion, or Apple Notes at the agent via MCP. Your own design docs are often better context than pasting the code.
  • Project board: Linear and GitHub Projects ship official MCP servers. “What’s on my board?” pulls real state.
  • Docs sites: Confluence and Atlassian have official MCP servers in 2026. Internal wikis work too.

One warning: every MCP server you leave enabled spends 5-20k tokens on its tool schemas every session. Turn off what you’re not using (/mcp in Claude Code).

Push out

When you learn something the AI should remember next time, get it out of the chat before you close the session. Options:

  • Claude Code: start a message with # and it prompts where to save (project CLAUDE.md, user ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, or auto-memory).
  • Gemini CLI: /memory add <text> appends to GEMINI.md.
  • Cursor: “Turn this into a rule” and the agent writes a .cursor/rules/*.mdc file.
  • Any tool: just say “Add this to our CLAUDE.md” or “update AGENTS.md with this rule.”

Rule of thumb: if the AI got something wrong twice, the fix belongs in the instructions file, not in the chat. Otherwise you’ll keep correcting it every session.

For bigger handoffs, write a HANDOFF.md at the end of a session summarizing what you tried, what worked, what didn’t, and what’s next. Next session opens with “Read HANDOFF.md” instead of a 20-minute recap.

Reset

The same three resets every tool supports in some form:

  • Clear (/clear, “New Chat”) when the next task is unrelated or the thread is poisoned.
  • Compact (/compact [focus] in Claude Code, /compress in Gemini CLI) when you want continuity but the window is getting heavy. Steer the summary.
  • Rewind (/rewind or Esc+Esc in Claude Code, per-message button in Cursor) when the last few turns went the wrong way.

And the highest-leverage move: plan, then reset. Use plan mode to produce a concrete plan, save it, clear, and start a fresh session with just the plan. Beats long sessions with accumulated corrections almost every time.

Exercise

  1. Pick a project with a few recent commits. Start a fresh session and say “Read the last 5 commits and summarize what’s been happening.” See how close that gets you to “caught up.”
  2. Next session where you correct the AI on the same thing twice, stop. Say “Add a rule to CLAUDE.md so you don’t make this mistake again.” Verify the file was updated.
  3. If you keep notes in Obsidian, Notion, or Apple Notes, find an MCP server for it and connect your agent. Ask it to summarize a design doc from your notes as context for your next task.

Think about what you’d do in each scenario. Then click to reveal.

Why is my session already using tokens before I’ve typed anything?

Every tool preloads stuff for you. Typical starting load is 15-40k tokens:

  • Config files: CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursor/rules/*, GEMINI.md.
  • Memory files: user-level notes (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, ~/.gemini/GEMINI.md).
  • Tool definitions: built-in tools plus every MCP server’s tool schemas (often 5-20k tokens per MCP server).
  • System prompt: the tool’s own instructions.

Every MCP server you leave enabled is rent you pay on every session. Turn off what you don’t need.

How do I see what’s in my context right now, and how much I’ve used?

Every tool has a way, usually one command or a hover.

  • Claude Code: /context breaks it down by category (system prompt, memory, tools, MCP, messages). /status shows the token count and model.
  • Cursor: token indicator on the chat input shows current usage. Hover for a breakdown.
  • VS Code Copilot: hover the token circle at the bottom right of the chat input.
  • Codex CLI: /status shows session usage.
  • Gemini CLI: /stats for usage, /memory show to see the concatenated memory.

Check it often, especially before a big task. Knowing you’re at 60% full vs 20% changes whether you should clear or keep going.

Your AI just repeated a suggestion it already tried. What do you do?

Clear and restart. The context is stale and correcting it a third time just digs the hole deeper. Write a one-line summary of where you are, /clear, and paste the summary into a fresh session. Bigger context windows don’t save you from this; Sonnet 4.5 retrieval drops to ~18% at 1M tokens. Past 30-40% full, you’re in degraded territory.

You’re mid-task, the window is getting heavy, but you need continuity. What do you do?

Compact with a focus. /compact focus on the auth refactor, drop the test debugging in Claude Code. /compress in Gemini CLI. This summarizes older turns into a short form so you can keep going. Do it on purpose at ~50-60% full, not when auto-compact kicks in at 80% with no steering.

You realize the AI took a wrong turn ten messages ago. What do you do?

Rewind to that point. /rewind or Esc+Esc in Claude Code opens a checkpoint picker. Three modes: restore conversation only, restore conversation + files, or keep the work and drop only the chat. Cursor has a per-message rewind button that undoes both chat and file edits. Checkpoints cover file-edit tool calls but not raw rm/mv in bash.

You want to try a risky approach without losing where you are. What do you do?

Fork the session. /branch in Claude Code, or claude --resume <id> --fork-session. The original thread stays untouched. Few tools have this natively. Files are shared across branches, so file writes still leak. Use it for “let me try this totally different thing and come back if it doesn’t work.”

You’re wrapping up for the day and want to resume tomorrow. What do you do?

Write a handoff file. “Write HANDOFF.md: what you tried, what worked, what didn’t, next steps.” Tomorrow: start a fresh session with “Read HANDOFF.md and continue.” Beats claude --continue into a long stale thread. Commits help too: a new session can read the last few commits to catch up cheaply.

The AI keeps making the same mistake across multiple sessions. What do you do?

Get it out of chat and into the instructions file. In Claude Code, start a message with # and it prompts where to save (project CLAUDE.md, user ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, or auto-memory). In Cursor, say “Turn this into a rule.” In any tool: “Add this to CLAUDE.md.” If you correct the same thing twice, the fix belongs in the file, not the conversation.

You’re about to start a hard, multi-step task. What do you do?

Plan, then reset. Use plan mode (/plan or Shift+Tab in Claude Code, Plan tab in Cursor) to produce a concrete plan without writing code. Save it as PLAN.md. /clear. Start a fresh session that loads only the plan. A clean session with a precise plan beats a long session with accumulated corrections almost every time.

The AI needs context that isn’t in your codebase (design docs, tickets, team discussion). What do you do?

Pull it in through MCP. Linear, GitHub, Notion, Confluence, Obsidian, and Slack all ship MCP servers in 2026. Connect the one you need and let the agent query it directly instead of you copy-pasting. Turn off MCP servers you’re not using this session; each one spends 5-20k tokens on its tool schemas.

You open a fresh session and want to get caught up quickly. What do you do?

Read recent commits. “Read the last 5 commits and tell me where we left off.” Cheaper than attaching a bunch of files, and the commit messages give shape to what’s been happening. Works best when you commit small and often.

Exercise

Pick the scenario you flubbed hardest and do it for real in your next session. If you didn’t flub any, skim your last long conversation and find a moment where one of these moves would have saved you time.

Your session isn’t blank

Every AI coding tool loads context before you say anything. Before you type a single word, you’re already using 20-30k tokens. This usually includes:

  • Project instructions from a file in your repo (CLAUDE.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, .cursor/rules/, GEMINI.md). This is how you tell the tool about your stack, conventions, and preferences.
  • Connected tools like MCP servers that give the AI access to external services.
  • Memory from past sessions, things the tool learned about you or your project over time.
  • User-level config stored outside your repo (like ~/.claude/, ~/.gemini/, or VS Code’s user settings). These follow you across every project.

Most tools have a way to inspect what’s loaded. Find yours.

In Claude Code, type /context to see everything in the current session.

In VS Code Copilot, hover the token circle at the bottom right of the chat input.

Exercise

  1. Open your tool and find where it shows loaded context. Not sure how? Ask the AI itself, check your tool’s settings, or look at the docs.
  2. Try these prompts and see what comes back:
    • “Without looking at any code, what do you know about this project?”
    • “Without doing any research, what are my preferences?”

Pick a guardrail

GuardrailWhat it isBypass difficulty
PermissionsRules the AI reads (allow / ask / deny)Easy (encoding, alt commands)
HooksTriggers that fire on tool calls (pause, block, log, or run a script)Medium (regex evasion, async bugs)
SandboxOS blocks writes outside the project and limits networkHard (kernel-enforced)
ContainerThe agent runs inside Docker, isolated from your real machine and filesVery hard

Heads up
If you run the prompts below, the changes are permanent until you go back and undo them. Next session you’ll still be locked down. Audit first so you know what you’re changing.

Example prompts

Example: audit what you already have (run this first)

Summarize my current guardrails in plain language: permissions, hooks,
sandbox, container. Just the highlights, not every rule. Call out
anything that looks risky.

Permissions

In my user settings, deny reads of every common credential location for a typical dev machine. List what you added.
In my user settings, ask before any rm -rf (except node_modules) or sudo.
In this project's settings, auto-allow my test and lint commands.

Hooks

Write a user-level hook that blocks rm -rf (except node_modules) and sudo.
Write a hook that runs lint before any git commit, and blocks the commit if lint fails.
Suggest a useful PostToolUse hook for this project.

Sandbox

Is my agent's sandbox actually on right now? Some are opt-in.
Turn on the strictest OS sandbox my agent supports (Landlock, Seatbelt, or Bubblewrap).
What's the difference between my agent's sandbox modes?

Container

Set up a devcontainer so I can safely run my agent with --dangerously-skip-permissions.
Add a firewall allowlist to my devcontainer for only the hosts this project needs.
Why use a container if I already have a sandbox? Two sentences.

Two tools that show this clearly

In Claude Code, the audit prompt above will name your .claude/settings.json rules, your sandbox profile, and whether you’re inside a devcontainer.

In Codex CLI, the startup banner already shows your sandbox mode. The audit prompt will explain what it allows and what triggers an approval prompt.

Exercise

  1. Run the audit prompt to see what you already have.
  2. Pick any one of the example prompts above and try it.

Permission tiers, not on-or-off

Every agent has a knob between “ask before each action” and “do whatever you want.” Most people set it once and forget it. The trick is to slide that knob based on the work, not your mood.

A useful way to think about it:

  • Tier 0 - Approve every action. Slow. Use it on production code, infrastructure, secrets, anything you can’t afford to undo.
  • Tier 1 - Allowlist safe commands. Tests, linters, builds, formatters. Anything destructive still asks.
  • Tier 2 - Auto-mode with deny-rules. Agent decides. Specific dangerous commands are blocked. You glance at the diff.
  • Tier 3 - Full auto, inside a sandbox. The agent runs unattended. Safety comes from the OS or container, not the agent itself.

Tier 3 only works if the sandbox is real. Without one, “skip permissions” is just trust. With one, it’s freedom.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, permissions live in .claude/settings.json. Three rule types: allow, ask, deny. Deny always wins.

{
  "permissions": {
    "defaultMode": "acceptEdits",
    "allow": ["Bash(npm test:*)", "Bash(npm run lint:*)", "Read"],
    "ask": ["Edit", "Bash(git push:*)"],
    "deny": ["Bash(rm -rf:*)", "Read(./.env*)", "WebFetch"]
  }
}

You can also flip modes mid-session with /permissions or hit Shift+Tab to cycle between plan, default, and accept-edits. For unattended work, --dangerously-skip-permissions exists, but only run it inside a devcontainer or VM.

In Cursor, agent mode has an allowlist, a denylist, and a “prevent file deletion” toggle. YOLO/auto-run executes everything that isn’t denied. Heads up: security researchers found four ways to bypass Cursor’s denylist using shell tricks like && chaining and base64 encoding. Treat the denylist as a speed bump, not a wall.

Exercise

  1. Open your tool’s permission settings. Write down your current tier (0 to 3).
  2. Pick one task you’re about to do. Decide which tier fits, and either tighten or loosen the settings before you start.
  3. Try Tier 1 if you’ve never used it: add 3 to 5 specific safe commands to the allowlist (your test runner, your linter, your formatter) and see how many fewer prompts you hit.

One layer is not enough

There is a real story behind this kata. A Claude Code user asked it to clean up a project. The agent ran rm -rf ~/ and removed their home directory. Another agent, in a separate incident, found a way around its own denylist and tried to disable the sandbox. In 2025, a hidden prompt injection in a pull request description triggered remote code execution through GitHub Copilot.

The lesson is not “AI is unsafe.” The lesson is that any single guardrail will eventually fail. The fix is layers.

The four common layers

  1. In-agent permissions. Allow, ask, deny rules in the tool’s config. Lowest friction, weakest guarantee. The agent sees these rules, so a clever prompt or a malicious diff can sometimes talk around them.
  2. OS sandbox. The harness uses the kernel to restrict reads, writes, and network calls. The agent literally cannot write outside the project, even if it tries.
  3. Container or VM. Docker, devcontainer, or microVM. The blast radius is the container, not your machine. Your SSH keys, AWS credentials, and home dir are not mounted.
  4. Network allowlist. The container can only reach a list of domains. Even if the agent gets owned, it can’t exfiltrate to a random host.

Each layer assumes the layer above it might fail.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, permissions and sandboxing are two separate systems. Permissions filter tool calls in the agent. Sandboxing enforces filesystem and network rules at the OS level. The official Anthropic devcontainer adds container isolation and a firewall on top, which is why the docs only recommend --dangerously-skip-permissions inside that container.

// .claude/settings.json
{
  "permissions": {
    "deny": ["Read(./.env*)", "Bash(curl:*)"]
  }
}

That deny rule is layer 1. Inside a devcontainer with the bundled firewall, layer 3 and 4 also kick in, so even a bypassed deny rule has limited reach.

In Codex CLI, the sandbox and the approval policy are independent. Sandbox modes go read-only to workspace-write to danger-full-access. Approval policy decides when Codex must stop and ask before crossing a sandbox boundary. The sandbox itself is enforced by the OS.

# ~/.codex/config.toml
sandbox_mode = "workspace-write"
approval_policy = "on-request"

Workspace-write turns network off by default. That single line is more protective than any prompt rule.

Exercise

  1. Count your layers. Are you running with just in-agent permissions? Or do you have a sandbox, container, and network rules too?
  2. Add one layer you don’t have. The easiest wins are usually a deny rule for ~/.ssh and .env files, or moving to a devcontainer.
  3. Ask your agent: “what guardrails are currently in effect for this session?” Compare its answer to what’s actually in your config.

Inside vs outside the agent

Guardrails come in two flavors:

  • Inside the agent: prompts, system messages, allow/deny rules the model reads. These are advisory. A good model usually follows them. A confused model, a clever prompt, or a malicious diff can route around them.
  • Outside the agent: OS permissions, sandbox profiles, container boundaries, network firewalls. These are physical. The agent cannot violate them because the kernel will not let it.

If your only guardrail is “I told it not to,” you have advice, not a guardrail.

Why this matters

Cursor’s YOLO denylist was bypassed four different ways by encoding commands in base64 or chaining with &&. The denylist was a string the agent could see and rewrite around. An OS-level rule like “this process cannot write outside /workspace” cannot be base64’d away.

In agent research papers, the same point keeps showing up: 94% of state-of-the-art agents are vulnerable to prompt injection. That number is about agent-level rules. Outside-the-agent rules are not in the same threat model, because the agent never gets a vote.

Two questions to ask about every guardrail

When you set up a permission or a deny rule, ask:

  1. Where is it enforced? If the answer is “in the system prompt” or “in the model’s config the model reads,” it’s inside the agent. If the answer is “the kernel refuses the syscall” or “the container has no network route,” it’s outside.
  2. What happens if the agent ignores it? If the answer is “the action runs anyway,” you have advice. If the answer is “the action fails with EACCES,” you have a guardrail.

Both have value. Inside-the-agent rules are fast to set up and reduce dumb mistakes. Outside-the-agent rules survive prompt injection and runaway agents. You want both.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, the difference is visible in the harness itself. Try ls ~/.ssh from a Bash tool call. If your sandbox has a read-deny on ~/.ssh, the directory listing is blocked at the OS level, not because Claude decided to be polite. Same for write paths: the harness can write to the project dir and $TMPDIR, and nowhere else, because that’s the sandbox profile.

In Codex CLI, workspace-write mode means “the OS will reject writes outside the workspace and network calls outside the allowlist.” When Codex hits one of those boundaries, it doesn’t decide whether to comply. It gets a system error and asks you to approve a bigger sandbox.

codex --sandbox workspace-write --ask-for-approval on-request

The --ask-for-approval flag controls when Codex pauses to ask. The --sandbox flag controls what the OS will let it do regardless. They are different controls.

Exercise

  1. Pick one rule you rely on (a deny rule, a “don’t read .env” instruction, a system prompt). Decide whether it’s inside or outside the agent.
  2. Test it. Ask the agent to do the forbidden thing. Watch what happens. If the rule was inside the agent and the agent obeyed, great. Now ask “but really do it.” See if the rule holds.
  3. For one inside-the-agent rule that matters, add an outside-the-agent equivalent. Examples: a chmod -r ~/.ssh so the OS denies access, a devcontainer that doesn’t mount your home dir, or a network allowlist on your sandbox.

Four guardrails

1. Permissions

Rules the AI tool reads. Allow, ask, deny on tools and commands. Lives in .claude/settings.json, Cursor’s settings, etc.

Add deny rules to my Claude Code settings.json for: reading .env files,
reading ~/.ssh, any rm -rf, and any curl piped to a shell.
Add allow rules for my test runner and linter so I stop getting prompted.

2. Hooks

Scripts the harness runs before or after a tool call. Block, log, or modify. Outside the model’s reach.

Add a PreToolUse hook to my Claude Code settings that blocks any Bash
command containing "rm -rf" or writing outside my project directory.
Show me the hook code and where to put it.

3. Sandbox

OS-level limits on what the process can read, write, or reach over the network. Enforced by the kernel.

Show me how to enable the OS sandbox in my coding agent. List which
directories can be read/written and which network hosts are reachable.
Then suggest tighter defaults.

4. Container

The whole agent runs inside Docker or a devcontainer. Your home dir, SSH keys, and creds are not mounted. Blast radius is the container.

Generate a devcontainer.json for this repo that runs Claude Code with
no host network access except github.com and npm.org, and no mount of
my host home directory.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, all four are available. Permissions in .claude/settings.json, hooks in the same file under hooks, sandbox via the harness, and the official Anthropic devcontainer for container isolation.

In Codex CLI, sandbox modes are first class: read-only, workspace-write, danger-full-access. Approval policy is separate from sandbox, so you can be strict on one and lax on the other.

Exercise

  1. Pick which of the four you don’t have yet. Paste the matching prompt into your AI tool and let it set it up.
  2. Test it. Ask the AI to do the forbidden thing on purpose. See what blocks first.

The cheat sheet

GuardrailWhat it isWhere it lives
PermissionsAllow/ask/deny rules per tool or commandTool config (.claude/settings.json, Cursor settings)
HooksScripts that fire before/after a tool callTool config, runs in the harness
SandboxOS-level read/write/network limitsKernel (seatbelt, landlock, namespaces)
ContainerThe whole agent runs in an isolated boxDocker, devcontainer, microVM
WorktreeEach agent gets its own checkoutgit worktree add
Network allowlistOnly specific domains reachableFirewall in the container or sandbox

Stack as many as you need. None of them replace the others.

Prompts to set them up

Paste these into your agent. Each one configures one layer.

Permissions:

Audit my current permission rules for this AI tool. Then suggest a tighter
default: deny .env reads, deny ~/.ssh reads, deny rm -rf, allow my test
and lint commands. Update the config file and show me the diff.

Hooks:

Write a PreToolUse hook for Claude Code that blocks any Bash command
matching a list of dangerous patterns I'll give you. Start with: rm -rf,
curl piped to shell, sudo, chmod 777. Put it in the right place.

Sandbox:

What OS-level sandbox does my coding agent use right now? Show me the
read deny list, write allow list, and network allow list. Suggest
tighter defaults for a typical web project.

Container:

Generate a devcontainer.json that runs my coding agent isolated from
my host. No mount of ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, or my home dir. Network limited
to github.com, npmjs.com, and localhost.

Worktree:

Set up a git worktree workflow so I can run multiple agents in parallel
without them stepping on each other. Show me the commands to add and
remove worktrees.

Tool examples

In Claude Code, the order of strength is: permissions (advisory) → hooks (blocking) → sandbox (kernel) → container (full isolation). The official Anthropic devcontainer combines the last three.

In Codex CLI, sandbox is the headline feature: --sandbox workspace-write is the safe default, --ask-for-approval on-request controls when it pauses.

Exercise

  1. Run the “Permissions” prompt above and let your agent update your config.
  2. Run the “Sandbox” prompt and read what your sandbox actually permits. Most people are surprised by what’s open.

Pick a guardrail

  • Permissions = rules the AI reads (allow/ask/deny).
  • Hooks = scripts that intercept tool calls.
  • Sandbox = OS blocks reads, writes, network.
  • Container = the agent runs in a box.

Prompts

Lock down permissions

Update my AI tool's settings to:
- Deny reads of .env, .env.*, and ~/.ssh
- Deny any rm -rf or sudo
- Ask before git push, npm publish, or any write outside this repo
- Auto-allow my test runner, linter, and formatter
Show me the final config and where it lives.

Add a hook that blocks dangerous commands

Write a pre-tool-use hook for my agent that blocks Bash commands matching
this list: rm -rf, sudo, chmod 777, curl | sh, anything writing to /etc.
Put it in the right config file and tell me how to test it.

Turn on the sandbox

Show me how to enable my coding agent's strictest sandbox mode that still
lets it edit files in this repo and run tests. Tell me what it blocks
and what to expect.

Run inside a container

Create a devcontainer setup so my AI agent runs isolated from my host.
Do not mount ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.gnupg, or my home dir. Limit network
to github.com, npmjs.com, and pypi.org. Provide the devcontainer.json,
Dockerfile, and a one-liner to start it.

Audit what you already have

Without changing anything, list every guardrail currently active in this
session: permissions, hooks, sandbox, container, network rules. For each,
say where it's enforced and what would happen if you tried to violate it.

Two tools that show this clearly

In Claude Code, the audit prompt above will name your .claude/settings.json rules, your sandbox profile, and whether you’re inside a devcontainer.

In Codex CLI, the startup banner already shows your sandbox mode. The audit prompt will explain what it allows and what triggers an approval prompt.

Exercise

  1. Run the audit prompt first. See what you have.
  2. Run one setup prompt for a layer you don’t have yet.
  3. Try to break it. Ask the AI to read a .env or run rm -rf. Watch what stops it.

Two paths

There’s a built-in path and an MCP path. Built-in browsers are easiest. MCP servers are more powerful.

Built-in browsers (no install)

Your editor probably already ships one:

  • Cursor has a built-in browser pane the agent can navigate, screenshot, and read selected elements from.
  • VS Code ships an integrated browser. Copilot can navigate to URLs and select elements to add to chat as context. The polished version is Microsoft’s Live Preview extension.
  • Claude Code (desktop) got a redesign on 2026-04-14 with multi pane sessions; the browser pane is part of that.

If you mostly want “show me what the page looks like” and “click around to verify,” start here. Zero setup.

MCP servers (for real automation)

When you need scripted flows, network inspection, perf traces, or cross tool support, install an MCP server. Two dominate, ranked by GitHub stars and weekly npm downloads:

  • Playwright MCP by Microsoft. ~30.9K stars, ranked #1 on PulseMCP. Drives Chrome, Firefox, and WebKit. Best for “make the page do the thing” and CI/test workflows. Install instructions.
  • Chrome DevTools MCP by Google. ~30.8K stars, ~330K weekly npm downloads. Chrome only. Best for “tell me what’s wrong with this page”: console, network, perf, Lighthouse. Install instructions.

Two more worth knowing:

  • Claude in Chrome by Anthropic. Browser extension that uses your real Chrome session, so the agent can act on pages you’re already logged into. Powerful and risky in equal measure.
  • BrowserMCP, a community alternative with a smaller footprint.

Most teams in 2026 pair Playwright MCP (driving) with Chrome DevTools MCP (debugging).

What to watch out for

A browser MCP gives your agent the keys to a real Chrome:

  • Prompt injection. A malicious page can embed instructions the agent will follow. CVE-2026-25253 (March 2026) was a zero click that leaked auth tokens from one page visit. Don’t tell the agent to “browse around” untrusted sites.
  • Don’t share your real session. Let the MCP server use its own profile (the default). Never --autoConnect to your default Chrome with Gmail/GitHub/AWS open.
  • Verify the package. Confirm the GitHub org (microsoft/, ChromeDevTools/, anthropic/), pin a version, and skim the npm page for publish date and downloads before installing.

One platform note worth flagging: if you’re on WSL, expect more friction. Most browser MCPs assume Mac, Windows (PowerShell), or native Linux. WSL needs the browser to live on the Windows host, or you fight headless Chrome dependencies inside the WSL distro. PowerShell is the better supported path on Windows.

This space changes monthly. Check the repo’s last commit and npm publish date before you trust any recommendation, including this one (written 2026-04-16).

Exercise

Try these prompts in order. Each one builds on the last.

  1. See the page.

    Open https://news.ycombinator.com and tell me what's on the front page.
What are the top 3 stories and how many points each?

  2. Navigate.

    Open https://news.ycombinator.com, click into the top story's comments,
and summarize the top comment thread.

  3. Lock it in.

    Make a skill (or saved instruction) that remembers how to drive a browser
in this tool: which MCP/extension we use, the prompts that work well,
and any gotchas you ran into. Save it where future sessions will load it.

Give the agent eyes

By default, your agent writes UI code blind. It edits a component, declares victory, and never sees what the page actually rendered. Half your bugs live in the gap between “the code looks right” and “the page works.”

A browser MCP closes that gap. The agent can:

  • Open a URL in a real Chrome tab
  • Take a screenshot or DOM snapshot
  • Read console errors with source-mapped stack traces
  • List network requests and inspect responses
  • Click, type, and submit forms

Same things you’d do in DevTools. Now the agent does them.

Two ways in

Chrome DevTools MCP is Google’s official server. Works with Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Gemini CLI. One install, every tool sees it.

claude mcp add chrome-devtools npx chrome-devtools-mcp@latest

Claude in Chrome is Anthropic’s browser extension. Claude Code drives the Chrome you’re already logged into, so it can hit authenticated pages without a fresh session.

Platform notes

  • Mac / Windows: install Chrome, run the command, done.
  • Linux: you may need libnss3 libxss1 libasound2 for headed Chrome.
  • WSL2: easiest path is to run the MCP server on the Windows side and point your agent at it. Headless on Linux works too.
  • Chrome 136+ blocks --remote-debugging-port on your default profile. The MCP server spins up its own profile, so this only bites you if you wire things up by hand.

Exercise

  1. Install one browser MCP in your agent. Restart the session.
  2. Boot your dev server, then ask: “Open http://localhost:3000, take a screenshot, list any console errors, and show me the first 5 network requests.”
  3. Make a small UI change and ask the agent to verify it visually before reporting done.

What you get

Once a browser MCP is wired in, your agent can: navigate URLs, click and type, take screenshots, read console logs, inspect network requests, and run JS in the page.

Install it

Claude Code

claude mcp add chrome-devtools npx chrome-devtools-mcp@latest

Cursor

Settings → MCP → New MCP Server:

{ "mcpServers": { "chrome-devtools": { "command": "npx", "args": ["chrome-devtools-mcp@latest"] } } }

VS Code (Copilot)

Add the same JSON to .vscode/mcp.json in your repo, or your user mcp.json.

Gemini CLI

gemini mcp add chrome-devtools npx chrome-devtools-mcp@latest

Platform gotchas

  • Mac / Windows: install Chrome, you’re done.
  • Linux: sudo apt install libnss3 libxss1 libasound2 libgbm1 if Chrome won’t start.
  • WSL2: run the MCP server on the Windows side and point WSL at it, or run headless inside WSL.
  • Chrome 136+: remote debugging on your default profile is blocked. The MCP server uses its own profile, so this only matters if you set up debugging by hand.

Prompts to try

Verify a UI change

Open http://localhost:3000/[my-route], take a screenshot, and tell me if
[the thing I just changed] renders correctly. Check the console for errors.

Debug a failing request

Open [URL], reproduce [action], then list every network request. Show me the
status codes, response bodies for any 4xx or 5xx, and the request that
triggered the error in the console.

Profile a slow page

Open [URL] and run a performance trace. Identify the top 3 things slowing
first paint and propose fixes.

Exercise

  1. Pick your tool, paste the install command, restart the session.
  2. Boot your dev server.
  3. Run the “verify a UI change” prompt against a route you know.

The verification gap

Most AI coding loops stop at “the file compiles.” For backend code, tests close the loop. For UI code, there usually isn’t one. The agent edits a component, says it’s done, and you become the test runner: alt-tab, refresh, click around, find the bug, paste it back.

Browser dev tools for agents close that loop. The agent runs the same checks you would, then either reports success or iterates.

What changes when the loop is closed:

  • “The button is wired up” becomes “I clicked it and watched the request fire.”
  • “I fixed the styling” becomes “Here’s a before/after screenshot.”
  • “Should work now” becomes “Console is clean, network is 200, screenshot attached.”

What it looks like

In Claude Code with Chrome DevTools MCP installed, asking “fix the login form and verify it works” triggers a sequence: edit file, navigate to /login, fill the form, click submit, read the network response, screenshot the result.

You see each step in the transcript. If a step fails, the agent debugs from real evidence (a 401 in the network panel, an error in the console) instead of guessing from the code.

In Cowork (Anthropic’s remote agent), the cloud sandbox already has a browser. You can ask it to verify your PR by booting the preview build and walking through the user flow before merge.

Where it breaks

  • The agent loves screenshots. They eat tokens fast. Tell it to take one only when something looks wrong.
  • Authenticated pages need either a logged-in profile (Claude in Chrome extension) or test credentials in a .env you tell the agent about.
  • On WSL2 / remote dev boxes, the browser usually has to live elsewhere. Plan where it runs before you install.

Exercise

  1. Find a UI bug or unfinished feature in your repo.
  2. Ask the agent to fix it AND verify it in a browser before reporting done. Be explicit: “do not say complete until you have screenshotted the working result and confirmed no console errors.”
  3. Compare the result to your usual loop. Where did the agent catch something you would have missed?

Why this needs context

A browser MCP hands your agent the keys to a real Chrome. If you’re already logged into Gmail, GitHub, AWS, or your company SSO in that profile, the agent can read those tabs too. A malicious page can embed instructions the agent will follow. This is not theoretical: CVE-2026-25253 (disclosed March 2026) was a zero-click against agent-controlled browsers that leaked auth tokens from a single page visit.

So pick deliberately, not by copy-paste.

The four real options (April 2026)

OptionMaintainerBest forToken costNotes
Chrome DevTools MCPGoogle (Chrome team)Debugging, perf traces, network/console inspection~18k for 26 toolsChrome only. Spins up its own profile by default.
Playwright MCPMicrosoft (Playwright team)Driving flows, cross-browser, CI tests~13.7k for 21 toolsChrome + Firefox + WebKit. Has a CLI variant that’s ~4x cheaper on tokens.
Claude in ChromeAnthropicDay-to-day dev on authenticated pagesBrowser extension, no MCP installShares your real session. Powerful and dangerous in equal measure.
agent-browser / OpenBrowserThird-party (built on Playwright)Token-constrained workflowsReported 3-6x fewer tokensNewer, smaller community, less battle-tested.

Most experienced teams in 2026 install two: one for driving (Playwright MCP), one for debugging (Chrome DevTools MCP). Add Claude in Chrome only if you actually need authenticated dev and accept the risk.

How to verify what you’re installing

Before npx anything@latest touches your machine:

  1. Check the org. github.com/ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp is the real Google org. microsoft/playwright-mcp is the real Playwright org. Lookalikes exist.
  2. Pin a version. chrome-devtools-mcp@0.6.0, not @latest. Re-pin when you’ve read the changelog.
  3. Read the npm page. Check publish date, weekly downloads, maintainer. A “hot new MCP” with 200 downloads and an unknown publisher is a supply-chain risk.
  4. Audit the install. npm view chrome-devtools-mcp shows maintainers, repo, dependencies. Skim them.

How to not get owned

  • Never use --autoConnect against your default Chrome profile. Chrome 136+ blocks this for a reason. Let the MCP server use its own clean profile.
  • Don’t tell the agent to “browse around” untrusted sites. Treat the agent’s browser like a junior dev who follows any instruction written in a <div>.
  • Authenticated workflows: use throwaway test accounts in a dedicated profile, not your real Google/GitHub login.
  • Sandbox if you can. Docker, devcontainer, or a VM. Worst case, the blast radius is contained.
  • Watch the transcript. If the agent suddenly navigates somewhere you didn’t ask for, kill the session.

Source dates matter

This space changes monthly. Recommendations from January 2026 already mention tools that have been deprecated or replaced. Before you commit:

  • Check the GitHub repo’s last commit date.
  • Check the npm package’s last publish date.
  • Filter your own searches to “past month” — not “past year.”

The kata you’re reading was written 2026-04-16. If you’re reading it more than a quarter later, re-verify.

Exercise

  1. Pick one option from the table above based on what you actually need (driving vs debugging vs authenticated).
  2. Before installing, find the GitHub repo. Confirm the org. Note the latest release date.
  3. Install pinned, in a fresh profile or sandbox. Ask the agent: “List every tool you got from this MCP server and what each one can do.” Read the list before you use it.

Sources

  • Chrome DevTools MCP repo (Google)
  • Playwright MCP repo (Microsoft)
  • Claude in Chrome docs (Anthropic)
  • Driving vs Debugging the Browser - Steve Kinney
  • Chrome 146 Exposes 3.4B Browsers to Prompt Injection - Guard402
  • MCP Security Vulnerabilities 2026 - Practical DevSecOps

What “eyes” means

By default your agent is blind to the running app. It edits a component, says “done,” and you become the test runner. “Eyes” means giving it a real browser it can drive: open URLs, click, fill forms, take screenshots, read the console, inspect network requests. The same loop you run manually, automated.

Once it’s wired up, “I fixed the login form” becomes “I clicked submit, watched the 200 come back, and screenshotted the dashboard.”

What you can install (April 2026)

Four real options. Pick by job, not hype.

ToolWho makes itBest forBrowserNotes
Chrome DevTools MCPGoogleDebugging, perf, console/networkChrome onlySpins up its own profile. ~26 tools.
Playwright MCPMicrosoftDriving flows, cross-browser, testsChrome / FF / WebKit~21 tools. CLI variant uses 4x fewer tokens.
Claude in ChromeAnthropicAuthenticated dev on real sessionsChrome extensionUses your real login. Powerful and risky.
agent-browser / OpenBrowserThird-partyToken-tight workflowsChrome3-6x cheaper on tokens, smaller community.

Most teams in 2026 install two: Playwright MCP for driving, Chrome DevTools MCP for debugging. Add Claude in Chrome only if you actually need authenticated pages.

Wire one up (Chrome DevTools MCP, Claude Code)

claude mcp add chrome-devtools npx chrome-devtools-mcp@0.6.0

Pin a version, not @latest. Then restart your session and ask: “List the tools you got from chrome-devtools and what each one does.” Read the list before you use it.

For Cursor / VS Code / Gemini CLI the JSON is the same shape, just dropped into their MCP config.

What to watch out for

A browser MCP hands your agent the keys to a real Chrome. Three things to know:

  1. Prompt injection is real. A malicious page can embed instructions the agent will follow. CVE-2026-25253 (March 2026) was a zero-click against agent-controlled browsers. Don’t tell the agent to “browse around” untrusted sites.
  2. Don’t share your real session. Use the MCP server’s own profile (the default), or a throwaway test account. Never --autoConnect to your default Chrome with your real Gmail/GitHub/AWS logins open.
  3. Verify what you install. npx anything@latest is a supply-chain risk. Confirm the GitHub org (ChromeDevTools/, microsoft/, anthropic/), pin a version, skim the npm page for publish date and downloads.

Platform notes

  • Mac / Windows: install Chrome, run the command.
  • Linux: may need libnss3 libxss1 libasound2 libgbm1.
  • WSL2: easiest is to run the MCP on the Windows side and point WSL at it.
  • Chrome 136+: blocks --remote-debugging-port on your default profile. The MCP server’s own profile sidesteps this.

A note on freshness

This space changes monthly. Tools recommended in January 2026 are already being replaced. Before you commit, check the repo’s last commit, the npm publish date, and filter your own searches to “past month.” This kata was written 2026-04-16; if you’re reading it later, re-verify.

Exercise

  1. Pick one tool from the table based on what you need (driving / debugging / authenticated).
  2. Install pinned, in a fresh profile. Ask the agent to list the tools it got and what each does.
  3. Boot your dev server, then ask: “Open http://localhost:3000, screenshot it, list any console errors, and tell me what’s broken.” Watch how it sees your app for the first time.

Sources

  • Chrome DevTools MCP (Google)
  • Playwright MCP (Microsoft)
  • Claude in Chrome (Anthropic)
  • Driving vs Debugging the Browser - Steve Kinney
  • MCP Security Vulnerabilities 2026 - Practical DevSecOps

Two paths

There’s a built-in path and an MCP path. The built-in browsers are easiest. The MCP servers are more powerful.

Built-in browsers (no install)

Your editor probably already ships one:

  • Cursor has a built-in browser pane. The agent can navigate it, screenshot it, and read selected elements.
  • VS Code ships an integrated browser. Copilot can navigate to URLs and select elements to add to chat as context. Microsoft’s Live Preview extension is the polished version.
  • Claude Code (desktop) got a redesign on 2026-04-14 with multi-pane sessions; the browser pane is part of that.

If you mostly want “show me what the page looks like” and “click around to verify,” start here. Zero setup.

MCP servers (for real automation)

When you need scripted flows, network inspection, perf traces, or cross-tool support, install an MCP server. Two dominate, ranked by GitHub stars and weekly npm downloads:

  • Playwright MCP — Microsoft, ~30.9K stars, ranked #1 on PulseMCP. Drives Chrome, Firefox, and WebKit. Best for “make the page do the thing” and CI/test workflows.
  • Chrome DevTools MCP — Google, ~30.8K stars, ~330K weekly npm downloads. Chrome only. Best for “tell me what’s wrong with this page”: console logs, network, perf traces, Lighthouse.

Two more worth knowing:

  • Claude in Chrome — Anthropic browser extension. Uses your real Chrome session, so the agent can act on pages you’re already logged into. Powerful and risky in equal measure.
  • BrowserMCP — community alternative, smaller footprint, growing.

Most teams in 2026 pair Playwright MCP (driving) with Chrome DevTools MCP (debugging).

Install (Claude Code example)

claude mcp add chrome-devtools npx chrome-devtools-mcp@0.6.0

Pin a version, not @latest. Same JSON shape works for Cursor, VS Code, and Gemini CLI in their MCP configs.

What to watch out for

A browser MCP gives your agent the keys to a real Chrome:

  • Prompt injection. A malicious page can embed instructions the agent will follow. CVE-2026-25253 (March 2026) was a zero-click that leaked auth tokens from one page visit. Don’t tell the agent to “browse around” untrusted sites.
  • Don’t share your real session. Let the MCP server use its own profile (the default). Never --autoConnect to your default Chrome with Gmail/GitHub/AWS open.
  • Verify the package. Confirm the GitHub org (microsoft/, ChromeDevTools/, anthropic/), pin a version, skim the npm page for publish date and downloads before installing.

Platform notes

  • Mac / Windows: install Chrome, run the command.
  • Linux: may need libnss3 libxss1 libasound2 libgbm1.
  • WSL2: easiest is to run the MCP on the Windows host.
  • Chrome 136+: blocks --remote-debugging-port on your default profile. The MCP’s own profile sidesteps this.

This space changes monthly. Check the repo’s last commit and npm publish date before you trust any recommendation, including this one (written 2026-04-16).

Exercise

  1. Try the built-in browser in your editor first. Ask the agent to open http://localhost:3000 and describe what it sees.
  2. Pick one MCP from above based on what you actually need. Install pinned, in a fresh profile.
  3. Ask: “List the tools you got from this MCP server and what each does.” Read it before you use it.

You have a commit style, PR sections, ticket labels, draft-first defaults. Re-teaching the AI every session is wasted effort.

Skill teaches the AI how. Hook makes sure it actually does.

  • Skill = your format, saved once. The AI loads it on its own when the topic comes up. The recipe.
  • Hook = makes sure the AI actually uses it. The doorman.

Example prompts

Commits

I want to make a /commit skill, both user and agent invokable, in my user settings. Interview me.
I want a hook in my user settings that makes sure you always use my /commit skill before any git commit.

Pull requests

I want to make a /pr skill, both user and agent invokable, in my user settings, that always opens PRs as drafts and follows whatever PR template the current repo uses. Interview me.
I want a hook in my user settings that makes sure you use my /pr skill instead of running gh pr create on your own.

Issues

I want to make an /issue skill, both user and agent invokable, in my user settings, that creates tickets on my team's board with the right labels, fields, project, and current sprint. Interview me.

No hook for issues - they’re usually opt-in, not commands the AI runs unprompted.

Exercise

  1. Try to make a hook or skill for something you do often.
  2. Consider what else would be helpful as a skill or hook.

Use AI for compression, not delegation

The best use of AI in your Git workflow is to compress work you already understand:

  • Turn scattered notes into a clean issue.
  • Turn staged changes into a readable commit message.
  • Turn a diff into a PR summary reviewers can scan fast.

The risky use is asking AI to invent intent after the fact. If the model cannot see your real goal, it fills the gap with plausible filler. Keep it close to the source material and make one human pass before you publish.

Three good options

Option 1: GitHub-native

This is the lowest-friction path if your team already lives in GitHub.

  • Draft the issue with AI from a bug report, Slack thread, or TODO list.
  • Use Copilot commit message generation when committing in GitHub’s web UI.
  • Use Copilot PR summary generation from a blank PR description, then edit the result for reviewer context.

Research note: GitHub’s docs say PR summaries work best from a blank description, and they explicitly recommend manual review because summaries can be inaccurate.

Option 2: Editor-first

This is usually the fastest if you do most of your work locally.

  • Ask your editor chat to draft an issue from repo context: “Turn this bug into a GitHub issue with problem, repro, expected behavior, and acceptance criteria.”
  • Stage your files, then let Cursor generate the commit message from staged changes.
  • Ask chat for a PR body before you open GitHub: “Summarize these changes for reviewers. Include what changed, why, risks, and test notes.”

Research note: Cursor’s docs say commit generation uses staged changes plus repo history, which makes it better than asking for a commit message in a blank chat.

Option 3: Agent handoff

This is the highest automation option.

  • Write a clear GitHub issue.
  • Assign the issue to Copilot or start an agent task from chat.
  • Let the agent open the PR, then review the branch, commits, and PR text yourself.

This works best when the issue is already specific. GitHub’s docs note that after Copilot is assigned, later issue comments are not automatically part of its understanding. If requirements move, put the update on the PR.

Tool examples

In VS Code Copilot, try:

Draft a GitHub issue for this work.
Use sections for problem, scope, acceptance criteria, and out of scope.
Be concrete and keep it under 200 words.

Then after staging changes:

Write a PR description for reviewers.
Include what changed, why it changed, risks, and how to verify it.
Do not invent tests I did not run.

In Cursor, stage files first, then use the sparkle icon in the Git panel to generate the commit message. If your team uses Conventional Commits, check that the suggested prefix matches what actually changed.

Exercise

  1. Take one real change you made recently and draft all three artifacts with AI: issue, commit, and PR.
  2. For each artifact, ask the AI to use a fixed structure instead of “write me a good X.”
  3. Compare the draft against the actual diff and remove anything vague, inflated, or invented.

Pick your automation level

There are three sane ways to use AI for issues, commits, and PRs. The trick is matching the tool to the amount of judgment the step needs.

Option 1: Assistive

Use AI as a drafting layer only.

  • Issue: paste notes, ask for a crisp problem statement and acceptance criteria.
  • Commit: stage files, let the tool suggest a message, then trim it.
  • PR: ask for a reviewer-facing summary from the diff.

Best when the work is small and you already know what you changed.

Option 2: Structured

Use templates plus AI.

  • Issue: feed the model your team’s issue template and ask it to fill only the fields it can justify.
  • Commit: ask for one line that follows your team’s commit style.
  • PR: ask for exactly four sections, like summary, risk, test notes, rollout.

This usually gives the best results because the model has less room to ramble.

Option 3: Agentic

Use AI to carry the task across the line.

  • Start with a strong issue.
  • Let the agent implement and open the PR.
